solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int countNumbers(string l, string r, int b) {
        const int MOD = 1e9 + 7;
        auto countUpTo = [&](string& s) {
            int n = s.size();
            vector<vector<int>> dp(n + 1, vector<int>(b, 0));
            dp[0][0] = 1;
            for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
                int digit = s[i - 1] - '0';
                for (int d = 0; d < b; ++d) {
                    dp[i][d] = dp[i - 1][d];
                }
                for (int d = 0; d < digit; ++d) {
                    dp[i][d] = (dp[i][d] + dp[i - 1][d]) % MOD;
                }
                dp[i][digit] = (dp[i][digit] + dp[i - 1][digit]) % MOD;
            }
            int total = 0;
            for (int d = 0; d < b; ++d) {
                total = (total + dp[n][d]) % MOD;
            }
            return total;
        };
        int left = countUpTo(l);
        int right = countUpTo(r);
        return (right - left + MOD) % MOD;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n * b), where n is the length of the input string and b is the base
space complexity: O(n * b), for the DP table
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to count the number of non-decreasing numbers up to a given string in base b. It calculates the count for both l and r and subtracts to get the result for the range [l, r]. The key strength is its efficient use of DP to avoid brute-force enumeration. The main weakness is the space usage, which could be optimized with a rolling array.
